title: The Rime of the Ancient Mariner — Killing the Daimon
subtitle: Coleridge's poem as the allegory of the soul that kills its own daimon and the long redemption that follows

summary.one_line: The Rime of the Ancient Mariner as the tragedy of the soul that kills its own daimon through ignorance, and the long purgation that restores the relationship.
summary.scope: Covers the albatross as the daimon killed by ignorance; the dead calm as stagnation of prāṇa; the spirit-ship and spirits that work the ship as the mantreśvaras; the blessing of the water snakes as recognition of the tattvas; the hermit as the guru; the mariner's compulsion to tell the story as the bodhisattva vow.

sources.label: Samuel Taylor Coleridge — The Rime of the Ancyent Marinere (1798) / The Rime of the Ancient Mariner (1817)

body.text: The albatross is the daimon — the guiding presence that appears at the moment of crisis (the ship trapped in ice) and leads the soul through the threshold ('Through the fog it came'). The mariner kills it 'with my cross-bow' — an act of ignorance that severs the connection between the soul and its guide. The cross-bow is significant: the instrument of killing is shaped like the cross, suggesting that the daimon is killed through the very structure of contracted consciousness (the 'I' that takes aim). The albatross hung around the neck is the weight of the severed daimonic connection — the guilt of having rejected one's own guidance.

The dead calm that follows the killing of the albatross is the stagnation of prāṇa — the breath/energy that moves the inner ship. 'Day after day, day after day, / We stuck, nor breath nor motion; / As idle as a painted ship / Upon a painted ocean.' The stasis is the experience of the soul cut off from its daimon: the energy does not flow, the water (the unconscious) is dead, and the sun (consciousness) becomes a curse. The rotting sea and the slimy creatures are the tattvas appearing in their impure, contracted form — experienced as repulsive because the unifying principle (the daimon) has been removed.

The spirit-ship that approaches without wind or tide is the operation of the mantreśvaras — the lords of the mantras who work the laws of the cosmos. Death and Life-in-Death (the Nightmare) gamble for the mariner's soul: Life-in-Death wins, condemning the mariner to a living death of endless wandering. The 'spirits that work the ship' after the mariner's blessing of the water snakes are the vidyeśvaras — the powers of knowledge that guide the ship when the soul aligns with the tattvas. The ship moves supernaturally fast, and the spirits speak in the mariner's ears — the śabda (sound) of the mantras themselves becoming audible.

The mariner's blessing of the water snakes is the turning point — the recognition of the tattvas in all forms. The mariner sees the snakes — slimy, repulsive creatures of the rotting sea — and suddenly sees their beauty: 'O happy living things! no tongue / Their beauty might declare.' This is the recognition that the tattvas, even in their most contracted and impure forms, are still expressions of the divine. The blessing happens involuntarily — 'I blessed them unaware' — because it is the action of grace (the śaktipāta) that operates through the soul when the contraction releases. The albatross falls from his neck at this moment: the daimon returns when the tattvas are recognized in all their forms.

The Hermit is the guru — the embodied guide who receives the mariner's confession and performs the absolution. The Hermit lives in the wood (the wild, natural state) and prays 'kneeling upon the sand' — the posture of the pure devotee. The mariner yearns for the Hermit's absolution: 'O shrieve me, shrieve me, holy man!' The Hermit is the guru who hears the confession (the acknowledgment of the killing of the daimon), but the absolution is not complete — the mariner must still carry the 'penance of ten years' in the form of the wandering. The guru opens the door but the soul must walk through.

The mariner's compulsion to tell his story is the bodhisattva vow — the inescapable drive to teach others what he has learned through suffering. The mariner must find the Wedding-Guest (the one who is about to enter the contracted state of marriage/attachment) and compel him to listen. This is the paunaruktya (the repetition) of the teaching — the story that must be told again and again until the lesson is learned. The mariner wanders from land to land and knows the faces of those who must hear: 'He hath the penance more, / And later to another shall / The mariner's tale be told.' This is the transmission of the teaching through the chain of those who have awoken.

gaps: The marginal gloss (1817 edition) needs analysis; 1798 vs 1817 versions need comparison; the Polar Spirit and the two voices need detailed tattvic mapping

issues.title: Passages need refinement with specific line references
issues.description: All passages currently summarize themes without precise line-number citations.

versions.summary: Initial RO created. Six passages covering the albatross as daimon, dead calm as prāṇa stagnation, spirit-ship as mantreśvaras, blessing of water snakes as tattva-recognition, hermit as guru, and mariner's vow as bodhisattva.
